posts.text: Uber just cut 23% of its People and Places division. The LinkedIn takes write themselves: "AI is coming for HR." Except Uber explicitly said AI wasn't a factor. Read the actual memo instead. Uber President Jill Hazelbaker's words: "HR was) too complex and fragmented, with overlapping responsibilities, unclear ownership, and teams operating too far from the businesses and partners they support." That memo describes an org design problem getting fixed in public. A few details worth sitting with: The cuts hit senior roles hardest. Uber still has 800+ open positions. Q1 gross bookings were up 25% year over year. A growing, profitable company looked at its HR function and decided it had grown in the wrong shape. Here's the uncomfortable part. When a People team gets cut while the business is booming, leadership is telling you they lost sight of what that team was connected to. The lesson for HR leaders is proximity and influence. Every layer between your team and the business it supports is a layer someone will eventually question. If you run a People function, ask yourself one thing: does every team I own have a clear line to a business outcome someone outside HR would name? If the answer is fuzzy, fix the structure before someone fixes it for you. What do you think actually drove this one: org complexity, cost discipline, or something else?
posts.hook: Uber just cut 23% of its People and Places division.
posts.name: Phil Strazzulla

posts.text: Calling all Boston based HR professionals! I'm hosting a breakfast at the WHOOP offices in Fenway on June 23rd and would love to see you there! This is an event for those who are interested in how HR can impact the P&L. We'll be talking revenue, EBITDA, and other financial metrics. But, we'll also keep it fun, with some great food, networking, giveaways (!!), and an event space that has truly remarkable views. Hope to see you June 23rd, you can RSVP in the first comment below!
posts.hook: Calling all Boston based HR professionals!
posts.name: Phil Strazzulla

posts.text: AI isn't accountable, doesn't get a bonus, and doesn't care about being fired (I hope). How do you think about managing a non-deterministic entity that doesn't care about the incentives used to manage humans through the many edge cases they encounter during work?
posts.hook: AI isn't accountable, doesn't get a bonus, and doesn't care about being fired (I hope).
posts.name: Phil Strazzulla

posts.text: What roles are OpenAI and Anthropic hiring for, and what does it imply about the future of work? Well, my friend Yash Ambardekar put together an analysis to answer just that! Some of the findings: - Of the >1K open roles, only 4 are entry level! - Sales and GTM are two of the biggest areas of hiring. It's a function of their growth, but also that large enterprise deals still need lots of humans - There are only 18 support roles, for products that serve hundreds of millions of users - Nearly 10% of roles relate to trust and safety, which I view as generally "AI oversight" If you want to explore, here is the data set: https://lnkd.in/ePXCRvSX Curious what stands out to you!
posts.hook: What roles are OpenAI and Anthropic hiring for, and what does it imply about the future of work?
posts.name: Phil Strazzulla

posts.text: Many organizations still treat employee disengagement as a mood issue rather than a systemic one. It is a common trap to rely on superficial perks like ping-pong tables instead of fixing the root causes. Consider these insights shared by Malvika Jethmalani on why current strategies often miss the mark: *   Systemic problems require structural solutions, not just individual interventions. *   70% of employee engagement variance is directly tied to the immediate manager. *   Engagement is a leadership responsibility, not just an HR department task. The focus needs to shift toward empowering line managers to cultivate better day-to-day environments. What is the single most effective way you have seen a manager improve team morale? #EmployeeEngagement #LeadershipDevelopment #WorkplaceCulture #ManagementTips #HRStrategy #BusinessGrowth
posts.hook: Many organizations still treat employee disengagement as a mood issue rather than a systemic one.
posts.name: Phil Strazzulla
